Eliminating Tawhid, proliferating axioms, and the slide into mysticism and totalitarianism Let us suppose a society fails to agree on a principle with an objective definition—one that can be stated explicitly as a Consistent formal axiomatic system—namely Tawhid, and that this principle is not taken as the primary reference for axiomatic reasoning. (The definition of God is objective in the sense that, within a mind-independent domain, it is graspable explicitly by saying not what God is but what certainly is not God; and we agree on the principle that no human being is God— that whatever God is, He is certainly not any of the humans or the living and non-living entities we see with our eyes.

Therefore, God has an objective, articulable definition that allows for distinctions in a formal language, a definition that is agreement-admissible regardless of whether you in fact agree to it or not or accept it or not.

By “agreement-admissible” I mean a logical property as used here; it does not mean you must accept it now; rather, it has an unambiguous definition.) Now, if a society refuses to accept Tawhid—with the objective clarifications given above (that we neither serve other humans nor anything we can see with our eyes; in other words, we do not serve what is not God, nor do we enslave anyone)—as a foundational axiom, then (which is to say, members of that society are implicitly declaring that it might be permissible to serve humans or other entities, or to enslave other humans!), in order to replace the unprovable yet agreement-admissible axiom of Tawhid, it will have to substitute a far larger number of unprovable yet agreement-admissible axioms (indeed, once Tawhid—defined as above—is removed, the number of such replacement axioms required becomes infinite!).

This, in turn, generates a great many shared legal disputes among people in a society, where each person clashes with another over why his proposition should count as the axiom and the other’s should not.

From any school of thought, philosopher, or theorist you like, ask for books, articles, and theories about human rights and the human sciences: they have not even provided a formal system; and the few who have, have not made it axiomatic; and the very few who offered axioms have produced systems that are not consistent and not Consistent.

Rationality requires that we seek, as far as possible, propositions that are provable, so that our lives are grounded in reasoning and argument, while we accept the smallest possible number of propositions without proof.
Every axiom is accepted without proof.

If two axiomatic formal systems are placed before me for the same purpose, and one requires accepting five unprovable premises while the other requires accepting fifteen, reason clearly dictates choosing the one with fewer unprovable premises.
What rational person would choose fifteen unprovable principles for the same goal?

Another point is that a large number of unprovable principles greatly increases the complexity of the system’s derived propositions—the rules or laws generated through logical inference from the axioms.
In the same way, the minimality of axioms reduces the complexity of a consistent axiomatic formal system—or of something like a legal code.

When the number of agreed but unprovable principles becomes large, the resulting laws and propositions become so numerous and complex that people increasingly depend on groups of interpreters, lawyers, and intermediaries.

Gödel, Philosophy of Mind, and the Rejection of the Idea that Humans Are Machines Gödel believed that concepts possess a realist form of existence.
Based on his own proven incompleteness theorems, he explicitly stated that in the philosophy of mind the human mind is not merely material.

According to Gödel, no Turing machine can ever be constructed that truly possesses a mind, even if it has extremely high complexity and exhibits abilities that appear far beyond those of a human being.
Gödel states explicitly that, on the basis of his incompleteness theorems, not only do other worlds exist, but we have either lived in them before or will continue our existence in them.
